Impulse Analysis
  • EDA
    • 1 · Dataset Level
    • 2 · Single Replay
    • 3 · Feature-Wise
    • 4 · Segments
  • Models
    • 5 · Baseline Models
    • 6 · Linear Model

On this page

  • Quarto
    • How it works
    • Installation
    • Project configuration: _quarto.yml
    • Freeze: the most important setting
    • Rendering
    • Adding a new notebook to the site
    • Notebook titles and structure
    • Publishing to GitHub Pages
    • What this looks like in an interview

Quarto

Quarto is a publishing system that converts Jupyter notebooks into polished HTML pages, PDFs, and multi-page websites. It is the primary way this project presents its analysis as a portfolio.

How it works

Quarto reads your .ipynb files (with all their already-computed outputs) and renders them into HTML. It does not re-run your notebooks by default — it just formats what is already there. This is critical for training notebooks where re-running would require a GPU and hours of compute.

The rendered output is a navigable website in _site/ that can be opened locally in any browser or deployed to GitHub Pages.

Installation

Quarto has two parts that are installed separately:

1. The Quarto CLI — the actual rendering engine. Install it once, globally:

brew install quarto

Verify it works:

quarto --version

2. The Python quarto package — already in pyproject.toml. This is a thin wrapper that lets you call quarto from Python if needed. You mostly won’t use it directly; the CLI is what you interact with.

Project configuration: _quarto.yml

The file _quarto.yml at the project root configures the entire site. Here is what each section does:

project:
  type: website         # render everything as a linked website
  output-dir: _site     # rendered HTML goes here (gitignored)

website:
  title: "Impulse Analysis"
  navbar:               # top navigation bar
    left:
      - text: "EDA"
        menu:           # dropdown with all EDA notebooks
          - ...
      - text: "Models"
        menu:           # dropdown with all model notebooks
          - ...

format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo        # clean Bootstrap theme
    toc: true           # table of contents on every page
    code-fold: true     # code is hidden by default; reader can expand it
    code-tools: true    # adds a toolbar button to show/hide all code

execute:
  freeze: auto          # see "Freeze" section below
  echo: true            # show code (before folding)
  warning: false        # suppress Python warnings in output

The code-fold: true setting is important for a portfolio: your plots and results are immediately visible, but an interviewer can expand the code with one click to see how you produced them.

Freeze: the most important setting

freeze: auto tells Quarto to never re-execute a notebook during rendering unless you explicitly ask it to.

When you first run quarto render, Quarto executes each notebook and saves the outputs in a _freeze/ directory alongside the source. On every subsequent render, it uses those saved outputs instead of re-running. This means:

  • quarto render is fast (seconds, not minutes/hours)
  • Training notebooks work fine — Quarto just displays the outputs already in the .ipynb file
  • Results are stable — a render today and a render next week produce identical output

When you want to re-run a notebook (e.g. after updating its code), clear its freeze entry:

# Re-run a single notebook on next render
quarto render notebooks/06_linear_model.ipynb --no-freeze

# Re-run everything (rarely needed)
rm -rf _freeze && quarto render

For training notebooks specifically, you will never clear the freeze — the notebook’s outputs come from a training run that happened externally, and those outputs are what you want to present.

Rendering

Render the entire site:

quarto render

This produces _site/index.html and all linked pages. Open _site/index.html in a browser to preview.

Render a single notebook:

quarto render notebooks/06_linear_model.ipynb

Useful when iterating on one notebook. The output goes to notebooks/06_linear_model.html (next to the source file).

Preview with live reload:

quarto preview

Opens a local web server and automatically re-renders when you save a notebook. Useful during active development. Note that with freeze: auto, previewing a notebook will re-execute it — so avoid using quarto preview for training notebooks.

Adding a new notebook to the site

  1. Create the notebook in notebooks/ and run it to completion in Jupyter.
  2. Add it to the appropriate menu section in _quarto.yml:
- text: "7 · PyTorch Linear Model"
  href: notebooks/07_pytorch_linear.ipynb
  1. Run quarto render to include it in the site.

Notebook titles and structure

Quarto automatically uses the first # Heading in your notebook as the page title. All existing notebooks follow this convention (e.g. # Next-frame prediction with a Linear Model), so no changes to the notebooks are needed.

If you want to add notebook-level settings that differ from the project defaults (e.g. a wider layout or a different theme for one notebook), add a raw cell as the very first cell of the notebook with YAML frontmatter:

---
title: "LSTM Model"
format:
  html:
    fig-width: 14
---

The cell type must be set to Raw in Jupyter (not Markdown or Code).

Publishing to GitHub Pages

This makes the site publicly accessible at https://<your-username>.github.io/impulse-analysis/.

# One-time setup: configure GitHub Pages to use the gh-pages branch
quarto publish gh-pages

Quarto handles everything: it builds _site/, pushes it to a gh-pages branch, and GitHub Pages serves it. After the first publish, subsequent updates are:

quarto render && quarto publish gh-pages

Add _site/ and _freeze/ to .gitignore so rendered output is not committed to main:

# .gitignore additions
_site/
_freeze/

The gh-pages branch is managed entirely by Quarto — you never need to touch it manually.

What this looks like in an interview

When showing the project to an interviewer:

  1. Send the GitHub Pages URL beforehand so they can browse it on their own machine. No Jupyter, no Python, no setup required on their end.
  2. Walk through the site in the order: dataset EDA → feature EDA → baseline models → linear model. The narrative builds naturally.
  3. Code is hidden by default — focus the conversation on findings and decisions. If they ask “how did you do that?”, expand the code with one click.
  4. For training jobs (LSTM, full-scale runs), the notebook shows the results without re-running anything. Point them to the W&B project page (see WANDB.md) for the full training history.
Source Code
# Quarto

Quarto is a publishing system that converts Jupyter notebooks into polished
HTML pages, PDFs, and multi-page websites. It is the primary way this project
presents its analysis as a portfolio.

---

## How it works

Quarto reads your `.ipynb` files (with all their already-computed outputs) and
renders them into HTML. It does **not** re-run your notebooks by default — it
just formats what is already there. This is critical for training notebooks
where re-running would require a GPU and hours of compute.

The rendered output is a navigable website in `_site/` that can be opened
locally in any browser or deployed to GitHub Pages.

---

## Installation

Quarto has two parts that are installed separately:

**1. The Quarto CLI** — the actual rendering engine. Install it once, globally:

```bash
brew install quarto
```

Verify it works:

```bash
quarto --version
```

**2. The Python `quarto` package** — already in `pyproject.toml`. This is a
thin wrapper that lets you call `quarto` from Python if needed. You mostly
won't use it directly; the CLI is what you interact with.

---

## Project configuration: `_quarto.yml`

The file `_quarto.yml` at the project root configures the entire site. Here is
what each section does:

```yaml
project:
  type: website         # render everything as a linked website
  output-dir: _site     # rendered HTML goes here (gitignored)

website:
  title: "Impulse Analysis"
  navbar:               # top navigation bar
    left:
      - text: "EDA"
        menu:           # dropdown with all EDA notebooks
          - ...
      - text: "Models"
        menu:           # dropdown with all model notebooks
          - ...

format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo        # clean Bootstrap theme
    toc: true           # table of contents on every page
    code-fold: true     # code is hidden by default; reader can expand it
    code-tools: true    # adds a toolbar button to show/hide all code

execute:
  freeze: auto          # see "Freeze" section below
  echo: true            # show code (before folding)
  warning: false        # suppress Python warnings in output
```

The `code-fold: true` setting is important for a portfolio: your plots and
results are immediately visible, but an interviewer can expand the code with
one click to see how you produced them.

---

## Freeze: the most important setting

`freeze: auto` tells Quarto to **never re-execute a notebook during rendering**
unless you explicitly ask it to.

When you first run `quarto render`, Quarto executes each notebook and saves the
outputs in a `_freeze/` directory alongside the source. On every subsequent
render, it uses those saved outputs instead of re-running. This means:

- `quarto render` is fast (seconds, not minutes/hours)
- Training notebooks work fine — Quarto just displays the outputs already in
  the `.ipynb` file
- Results are stable — a render today and a render next week produce identical
  output

**When you want to re-run a notebook** (e.g. after updating its code), clear
its freeze entry:

```bash
# Re-run a single notebook on next render
quarto render notebooks/06_linear_model.ipynb --no-freeze

# Re-run everything (rarely needed)
rm -rf _freeze && quarto render
```

For training notebooks specifically, you will never clear the freeze — the
notebook's outputs come from a training run that happened externally, and those
outputs are what you want to present.

---

## Rendering

**Render the entire site:**

```bash
quarto render
```

This produces `_site/index.html` and all linked pages. Open `_site/index.html`
in a browser to preview.

**Render a single notebook:**

```bash
quarto render notebooks/06_linear_model.ipynb
```

Useful when iterating on one notebook. The output goes to
`notebooks/06_linear_model.html` (next to the source file).

**Preview with live reload:**

```bash
quarto preview
```

Opens a local web server and automatically re-renders when you save a notebook.
Useful during active development. Note that with `freeze: auto`, previewing a
notebook will re-execute it — so avoid using `quarto preview` for training
notebooks.

---

## Adding a new notebook to the site

1. Create the notebook in `notebooks/` and run it to completion in Jupyter.
2. Add it to the appropriate `menu` section in `_quarto.yml`:

```yaml
- text: "7 · PyTorch Linear Model"
  href: notebooks/07_pytorch_linear.ipynb
```

3. Run `quarto render` to include it in the site.

---

## Notebook titles and structure

Quarto automatically uses the first `# Heading` in your notebook as the page
title. All existing notebooks follow this convention (e.g. `# Next-frame
prediction with a Linear Model`), so no changes to the notebooks are needed.

If you want to add notebook-level settings that differ from the project
defaults (e.g. a wider layout or a different theme for one notebook), add a
raw cell as the **very first cell** of the notebook with YAML frontmatter:

```yaml
---
title: "LSTM Model"
format:
  html:
    fig-width: 14
---
```

The cell type must be set to **Raw** in Jupyter (not Markdown or Code).

---

## Publishing to GitHub Pages

This makes the site publicly accessible at
`https://<your-username>.github.io/impulse-analysis/`.

```bash
# One-time setup: configure GitHub Pages to use the gh-pages branch
quarto publish gh-pages
```

Quarto handles everything: it builds `_site/`, pushes it to a `gh-pages`
branch, and GitHub Pages serves it. After the first publish, subsequent
updates are:

```bash
quarto render && quarto publish gh-pages
```

Add `_site/` and `_freeze/` to `.gitignore` so rendered output is not
committed to `main`:

```
# .gitignore additions
_site/
_freeze/
```

The `gh-pages` branch is managed entirely by Quarto — you never need to touch
it manually.

---

## What this looks like in an interview

When showing the project to an interviewer:

1. **Send the GitHub Pages URL** beforehand so they can browse it on their own
   machine. No Jupyter, no Python, no setup required on their end.
2. **Walk through the site** in the order: dataset EDA → feature EDA → baseline
   models → linear model. The narrative builds naturally.
3. **Code is hidden by default** — focus the conversation on findings and
   decisions. If they ask "how did you do that?", expand the code with one
   click.
4. **For training jobs** (LSTM, full-scale runs), the notebook shows the
   results without re-running anything. Point them to the W&B project page
   (see `WANDB.md`) for the full training history.
